title: Reading and Use of English - Part 6
description: You are going to read a magazine article about an intellectual process known as critical thinking. For questions 31 – 36, choose the answer (A, B, C or D) which you think fits best according to the text. Mark your answers on the separate answer sheet.

texts.A: [37]The rise of direct-to-consumer genetic testing is a privacy nightmare disguised as a voyage of self-discovery.[/37] [38]When you hand over your DNA to a private corporation, you are not just compromising your own data, but that of your relatives who never consented to the test.[/38] [39]These databases are a goldmine for insurance companies and law enforcement, and no amount of 'anonymization' can truly protect the unique blueprint of a human being.[/39] We are selling our most intimate biological secrets for the price of a novelty ancestry report.
texts.B: Genetic testing has revolutionized our understanding of health and heritage, empowering individuals to take a proactive role in their own wellbeing. By identifying predispositions to certain conditions, users can make lifestyle changes that save lives. [37]The privacy concerns are largely overblown; most companies have strict protocols and only share data with researchers when explicit consent is given.[/37] To block this technology would be to deny millions of people access to life-saving information and a deeper connection to their ancestral history.
texts.C: [40]I agree that the health insights provided by these tests are a significant benefit, but we must address the 'biological determinism' they promote.[/40] A 2% predisposition to a disease is not a destiny, yet many users experience unnecessary anxiety or make radical medical decisions based on misunderstood data. I share the view that the lack of regulation regarding secondary data use is a major flaw. [38]We need a 'Genetic Bill of Rights' that ensures our DNA cannot be used against us by employers or insurers, regardless of the fine print on a website.[/38]
texts.D: The most troubling aspect of the DNA testing boom is the commodification of identity. Identity is a social and cultural construct, not a percentage in a tube. By reducing ourselves to '40% Scandinavian' or '10% West African,' we are reinforcing old racial categories that have no basis in modern science. [39]While I share the concern about law enforcement access to these databases, I believe the real harm is the way these tests distract us from the lived reality of our communities.[/39] [40]We are looking into the past to avoid the complexities of the present.[/40]

questions.question: Which expert has a different view from B on the severity of the privacy risks associated with DNA testing?
questions.explanation: Expert B says privacy concerns are 'largely overblown.' Expert A calls it a 'privacy nightmare' and says 'no amount of anonymization' can protect the data.

questions.question: Which expert shares A's opinion about the potential for third-party organizations (like insurers) to exploit genetic data?
questions.explanation: Expert A mentions these databases are a 'goldmine for insurance companies.' Expert C also warns that DNA could be 'used against us by employers or insurers.'

questions.question: Which expert expresses a similar view to D regarding the misuse of genetic data by the authorities?
questions.explanation: Expert D shares the concern about 'law enforcement access to these databases.' Expert A also explicitly lists 'law enforcement' as one of the groups for whom these databases are a goldmine.

questions.question: Which expert has a different view from C on the primary value of the health information these tests provide?
questions.explanation: Expert C believes the health insights are a 'significant benefit.' Expert D, however, argues that the focus on such data is a 'distraction' from 'lived reality' and reinforces problematic categories.
